stem: In a conventional spin-echo sequence, when is the 180-degree pulse applied relative to the echo?

options.text: At the same instant as the 90-degree excitation
After the echo has already been sampled
Halfway between the 90-degree excitation and the echo
One full repetition time before excitation

explanation: The 180-degree pulse is applied at TE/2 so that static frequency offsets rephase at TE. This timing creates the spin echo while preserving irreversible T2 decay.

objective: Distinguish spin echo from gradient echo.
stem: What is the principal purpose of the 180-degree pulse in a spin-echo sequence?

options.text: To restore signal lost through irreversible T2 interactions
To select the phase-encoding matrix
To create a permanent increase in B0
To refocus dephasing caused by static field inhomogeneity

explanation: The refocusing pulse reverses phase dispersion from stable frequency differences, producing an echo governed more closely by true T2. It cannot reverse microscopic spin-spin relaxation.

stem: How does a multiecho spin-echo sequence obtain signals at more than one TE after a single excitation?

options.text: It applies multiple 180-degree refocusing pulses
It applies multiple slice-selection gradients without refocusing
It changes the main field between receiver samples
It uses one RF pulse and records only the free induction decay

explanation: Repeated refocusing pulses generate a train of spin echoes at progressively longer echo times. Those echoes can provide different T2 weighting or populate multiple k-space lines.

stem: How can multiple slices be acquired within one repetition interval?

options.text: Every slice is assigned the same RF center frequency
Excitations and echoes for different slices are interleaved while other slices recover
The slice gradient is omitted after the first slice
All slices are compressed into one phase-encoding line

explanation: After one slice is excited, the system can excite and collect data from other slices during otherwise unused recovery time. Slice-selective frequencies and gradients keep the signals spatially distinct.

stem: How does fast spin echo shorten acquisition compared with conventional spin echo?

options.text: One echo is copied into every k-space line
The phase-encoding gradient is eliminated
Multiple echoes after one excitation fill multiple k-space lines
All 180-degree pulses are replaced by a longer TR

explanation: An echo train uses a different phase-encoding value for each refocused echo, collecting several lines per TR. The echo train length therefore reduces the number of required repetitions.

objective: Define effective TE in fast spin echo.
stem: What determines the effective TE of a fast spin-echo image?

options.text: The time of the final echo regardless of k-space order
The interval between adjacent slice excitations
The duration of the receiver's first dwell interval
The echo time of the signal used to fill central k-space

explanation: Contrast is dominated by central k-space, so the echo assigned there defines effective TE. Other echoes fill more peripheral lines and influence detail and blurring.
